questions.question: Which two attributes define the completion state of a <progress> element?
questions.explanation: The value attribute indicates the current progress and max defines the total work. Together they represent completion as value/max.

questions.question: What happens when you omit the value attribute from a <progress> element?
choices: The progress bar displays as indeterminate
The element is invalid and will not render
questions.explanation: A <progress> element without a value attribute renders as an indeterminate progress bar, typically shown as an animated bar indicating that work is in progress but the completion amount is unknown.

questions.question: What is the default value of the max attribute on a <progress> element if it is not specified?
questions.explanation: When the max attribute is omitted, it defaults to 1.0. So a value of 0.5 would represent 50% completion.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly creates a progress bar showing 70% completion?
questions.explanation: Setting value="70" and max="100" creates a determinate progress bar at 70%. Option A would also work but would show 70 out of a default max of 1, which is clamped to 100%.

questions.question: The HTML tag used to represent the completion progress of a task is ___.
questions.explanation: The <progress> element displays a progress bar indicating how much of a task has been completed.

questions.question: A progress bar without a ___ attribute is considered indeterminate.
questions.explanation: When the value attribute is omitted, the browser cannot determine the completion percentage and renders an indeterminate (animated) progress indicator.
